quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Rate law"?

options.text: Acid: produces H⁺(aq); base: produces OH⁻(aq); limited to aqueous solutions
Properties that depend on the number of dissolved particles, not their chemical identity: ΔTb, ΔTf, π, ΔP
When a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts in the direction that partially counteracts the disturbance
An equation relating reaction rate to reactant concentrations: rate = k[A]ᵐ[B]ⁿ; exponents determined experimentally

quiz_questions.explanation: "Rate law" is defined as: An equation relating reaction rate to reactant concentrations: rate = k[A]ᵐ[B]ⁿ; exponents determined experimentally. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Reaction order"?

options.text: The exponent of a concentration term in the rate law; indicates how rate responds to concentration changes (0th, 1st, 2nd)
-log[H⁺]; a logarithmic scale where each unit represents a 10-fold change in [H⁺]; pH 3 is 1000× more acidic than pH 6
A mixture of two or more metals (or a metal and nonmetal); substitutional alloys have similar-sized atoms, interstitial alloys have small atoms in gaps

A weak acid or base that changes color at a specific pH range; chosen so its color change range includes the equivalence point pH (e.g., phenolphthalein, pH 8.2–10, ideal for weak acid–strong base titrations; bromothymol blue, pH 6.0–7.6, for...

quiz_questions.explanation: "Reaction order" is defined as: The exponent of a concentration term in the rate law; indicates how rate responds to concentration changes (0th, 1st, 2nd). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Rate constant (k)"?

options.text: The entropy of a perfect crystal at absolute zero (0 K) is zero
The temperature and pressure at which all three phases coexist in equilibrium (water: 0.01°C, 611.7 Pa)
The proportionality constant in the rate law; units depend on overall order; increases exponentially with temperature
When a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts in the direction that partially counteracts the disturbance

quiz_questions.explanation: "Rate constant (k)" is defined as: The proportionality constant in the rate law; units depend on overall order; increases exponentially with temperature. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Integrated rate law"?

options.text: Loss of electrons; occurs at the anode; oxidation state increases
The ratio of product to reactant concentrations at equilibrium, each raised to their stoichiometric coefficients; temperature-dependent
A model stating reactions occur when molecules collide with sufficient energy (≥ Ea) and proper orientation
Relates concentration to time: zero-order [A] = -kt + [A]₀; first-order ln[A] = -kt + ln[A]₀; second-order 1/[A] = kt + 1/[A]₀

quiz_questions.explanation: "Integrated rate law" is defined as: Relates concentration to time: zero-order [A] = -kt + [A]₀; first-order ln[A] = -kt + ln[A]₀; second-order 1/[A] = kt + 1/[A]₀. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Half-life"?

options.text: Loss of electrons; occurs at the anode; oxidation state increases
Time for [A] to decrease to half its initial value: t₁/₂ = [A]₀/2k (zero), 0.693/k (first, constant), 1/k[A]₀ (second)
A model stating reactions occur when molecules collide with sufficient energy (≥ Ea) and proper orientation
π = iMRT; the pressure needed to prevent osmosis across a semipermeable membrane; used to determine molar mass of large molecules

quiz_questions.explanation: "Half-life" is defined as: Time for [A] to decrease to half its initial value: t₁/₂ = [A]₀/2k (zero), 0.693/k (first, constant), 1/k[A]₀ (second). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "An equation relating reaction rate to reactant concentrations: rate = k[A]ᵐ[B]ⁿ; exponents determined experimentally"?

options.text: Stoichiometric conversion
Side reaction
Rate law
ΔS prediction rules

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Rate law".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The exponent of a concentration term in the rate law; indicates how rate responds to concentration changes (0th, 1st, 2nd)"?

options.text: Equilibrium vs. completion
Catalyst effect
Factors affecting rate
Reaction order

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Reaction order".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The proportionality constant in the rate law; units depend on overall order; increases exponentially with temperature"?

options.text: Selective precipitation
Pressure/volume change
Rate constant (k)
Stoichiometric conversion

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Rate constant (k)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Relates concentration to time: zero-order [A] = -kt + [A]₀; first-order ln[A] = -kt + ln[A]₀; second-order 1/[A] = kt + 1/[A]₀"?

options.text: Solubility product (Ksp)
Integrated rate law
Intermediate
Aufbau principle

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Integrated rate law".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Time for [A] to decrease to half its initial value: t₁/₂ = [A]₀/2k (zero), 0.693/k (first, constant), 1/k[A]₀ (second)"?

options.text: Aufbau principle
Half-life
Nernst equation
Spectator ion

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Half-life".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Rate Laws and Reaction Order is accurate?

options.text: Adding reactant or removing product shifts equilibrium forward (toward products); adding product or removing reactant shifts it in reverse.
Lewis structures show bonding and lone pairs, with formal charge minimization guiding the best structure.
Strong acids (HCl, HBr, HI, H₂SO₄, HNO₃, HClO₄) and strong bases (group 1/2 hydroxides) dissociate completely.
Rate Laws and Reaction Order describe the mathematical relationship between reaction rate and reactant concentrations.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Rate Laws and Reaction Order as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Rate Laws and Reaction Order?

options.text: Reactions with ΔG°  1) are spontaneous under standard conditions.
Chemical Equilibrium occurs when a reversible reaction's forward and reverse rates are equal, resulting in constant concentrations of reactants and products.
Periodic Table Trends arise from systematic changes in electron configuration across periods and groups.
For aA + bB → products, rate = k[A]ᵐ[B]ⁿ, where m and n are reaction orders (determined experimentally, NOT from coefficients).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Rate Laws and Reaction Order as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Rate Laws and Reaction Order?

options.text: Adding reactant or removing product shifts equilibrium forward (toward products); adding product or removing reactant shifts it in reverse.
The overall order = m + n.
Conjugate acid-base pairs differ by one H⁺.
Dynamic Equilibrium is reached when a reversible reaction's forward and reverse rates become equal, with no net change in concentrations.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Rate Laws and Reaction Order as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Rate Laws and Reaction Order?

options.text: Strong acids (HCl, HBr, HI, H₂SO₄, HNO₃, HClO₄) and strong bases (group 1/2 hydroxides) dissociate completely.
The choice of method depends on the properties of the mixture components and the purity required.
The Nernst equation adjusts E for non-standard conditions: E = E° - (RT/nF)ln Q.
Zero-order: rate is independent of concentration; first-order: rate doubles when concentration doubles; second-order: rate quadruples when concentration doubles.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Rate Laws and Reaction Order as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Rate Laws and Reaction Order?

options.text: The rate constant k varies with temperature.

Acid-Base Definitions have evolved from Arrhenius (acids produce H⁺, bases produce OH⁻ in water) to Brønsted-Lowry (acids donate H⁺, bases accept H⁺) to Lewis (acids accept electron pairs, bases donate electron pairs).
Colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure, vapor pressure lowering) depend on solute particle concentration, not identity.
Dynamic Equilibrium is reached when a reversible reaction's forward and reverse rates become equal, with no net change in concentrations.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Rate Laws and Reaction Order as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Rate Laws and Reaction Order?

options.text: Kidney dialysis using osmotic pressure principles to remove waste from blood across a semipermeable membrane
Hard water treatment: Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions forming insoluble CaCO₃ scale in pipes and water heaters when Qsp exceeds Ksp upon heating
Blood buffering: CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ ⇌ HCO₃⁻ + H⁺ with specific K values maintaining blood pH in the narrow range of 7.35-7.45
Radioactive decay following first-order kinetics with constant half-life: carbon-14 (t₁/₂ = 5,730 years) used for archaeological dating

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Rate Laws and Reaction Order.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Rate Laws and Reaction Order?

options.text: Kidney dialysis using osmotic pressure principles to remove waste from blood across a semipermeable membrane
Drug elimination from the body often following first-order kinetics: dosing intervals based on half-life to maintain therapeutic blood levels
Hard water treatment: Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions forming insoluble CaCO₃ scale in pipes and water heaters when Qsp exceeds Ksp upon heating
Radiocarbon dating using the ¹⁴C isotope to determine the age of organic materials up to ~50,000 years old

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Rate Laws and Reaction Order.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Rate Laws and Reaction Order applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Aluminum's amphoteric nature: Al(OH)₃ dissolves in both acids and bases, exploited in the Bayer process to purify alumina from bauxite ore
Shelf life determination of food and pharmaceuticals using rate laws to predict how quickly products degrade at different storage temperatures
Ammonia synthesis economics: K for N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ is large at low T (favors products) but reaction is too slow; industrial compromise at ~450°C, 200 atm gives usable rate and yield
Industrial ammonia production optimizing the ratio of N₂ to H₂ to minimize waste and maximize yield in the Haber process

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Rate Laws and Reaction Order.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Rate Laws and Reaction Order?

options.text: Diamond vs. graphite: same element (carbon) with different covalent bonding networks—diamond (4 bonds, 3D tetrahedral) is the hardest material, graphite (3 bonds, layered sheets) is a lubricant
Water treatment using selective precipitation: adding Na₂S to remove toxic heavy metals (Pb²⁺, Hg²⁺, Cu²⁺) as insoluble sulfide precipitates (very low Ksp values)
The spontaneous rusting of iron: despite being exothermic (ΔH < 0), rust formation is also driven by the entropy increase from solid Fe converting to flaky Fe₂O₃
Radioactive decay following first-order kinetics with constant half-life: carbon-14 (t₁/₂ = 5,730 years) used for archaeological dating

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Rate Laws and Reaction Order.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Rate Laws and Reaction Order in practice?

options.text: Breathalyzer devices using electrochemical oxidation of ethanol to acetic acid, generating a current proportional to blood alcohol concentration
Quality control in manufacturing: monitoring percent yield to detect equipment problems, contaminated reagents, or process deviations
Drug elimination from the body often following first-order kinetics: dosing intervals based on half-life to maintain therapeutic blood levels
Ammonia synthesis economics: K for N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ is large at low T (favors products) but reaction is too slow; industrial compromise at ~450°C, 200 atm gives usable rate and yield

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Rate Laws and Reaction Order.
